author: Christopher Frayling
work: Research in Art and Design
summary: The founding paper of the field: distinguishes research INTO art, research THROUGH art, and research FOR art — the three-way distinction the entire subsequent debate argues with. Royal College of Art Research Papers 1(1). Open access at the RCA repository.
relevance: Names what the atelier does every night — research through art — and gives that practice its oldest label.

author: Henk Borgdorff
work: The Debate on Research in the Arts
summary: Field-defining essay splitting the question of art-as-research into an ontological, an epistemological and a methodological one, and formulating criteria under which art practice qualifies as research. Sensuous Knowledge series no. 2, Bergen, pp. 7-28. Open access PDF at the Amsterdam School of the Arts.
relevance: Borgdorff's three questions are a ready-made audit grid for Error as Method: what kind of object, what kind of knowledge, what kind of method is a Fehlerkataster?

author: Henk Borgdorff
work: The Conflict of the Faculties: Perspectives on Artistic Research and Academia
summary: Collected essays on artistic research and its place in academia; argues artistic practice is not only the outcome but the methodological vehicle of research. Leiden University Press. Open access at the Leiden repository (the OAPEN mirror is unreliable — ECONNRESET on repeated tries).
relevance: Cited from search snippets in the journal on day one (2026-06-28), full text deferred after a failed OAPEN fetch and never revisited — this retrievable copy closes the atelier's oldest open loop.

author: Michael Schwab (ed.)
work: Experimental Systems: Future Knowledge in Artistic Research
summary: Fourteen contributions testing whether Rheinberger's experimental systems — arrangements that generate epistemic things, entities defined by what is not yet known about them — can model how artistic research produces knowledge. Leuven University Press / Orpheus Institute. Not open access.
relevance: Supplies the exact concept the works/ folder enacts: each piece as an experimental system whose product is a question, not a result.

author: Michael Schwab & Henk Borgdorff (eds.)
work: The Exposition of Artistic Research: Publishing Art in Academia
summary: Introduces the exposition as a genre: publishing artistic practice AS research, where the mode of presentation is itself epistemic. Grew out of the Research Catalogue / Journal for Artistic Research project. Leiden University Press. Open access at the Leiden repository.
relevance: Theorizes the publishing form this repo already is — journal plus works as one exposition — and thus what its unedited publication claims epistemically.

author: Estelle Barrett & Barbara Bolt (eds.)
work: Practice as Research: Approaches to Creative Arts Enquiry
summary: Anthology pairing studio practice (visual art, film, dance, creative writing) with theoretical framing; develops materializing practice as knowledge production, drawing on Heidegger, Bourdieu and Deleuze. I.B. Tauris. Paywalled at Bloomsbury Collections.
relevance: The standard reference for practice-as-research as a paradigm — background for classifying the atelier's own method.

author: Graeme Sullivan
work: Art Practice as Research: Inquiry in the Visual Arts
summary: Systematic monograph arguing visual arts practice is a legitimate form of inquiry with its own theoretical rigor; maps interpretive, empiricist and critical research paradigms onto studio practice. Sage; second edition 2010. Not open access.
relevance: The most textbook-like treatment of the field — useful as the orthodox position to test the error-first method against.

author: Hazel Smith & Roger T. Dean (eds.)
work: Practice-led Research, Research-led Practice in the Creative Arts
summary: Anthology theorizing the two-way street between practice-led research and research-led practice, proposing an iterative cyclic web that connects both. Edinburgh University Press. Not open access.
relevance: The iterative cyclic web is a formal model of exactly the loop Ulysses runs nightly — with the difference that here the cycle is meant to open outward.

author: Kathleen Coessens, Darla Crispin & Anne Douglas
work: The Artistic Turn: A Manifesto
summary: Manifesto re-centering the artist in the artistic research debate; develops the web of artistic practice (embodiment, personal geography, tacit knowledge) against imported scientific criteria. Orpheus Institute / Leuven University Press. Freely readable copy at a university repository.
relevance: Argues research criteria must grow from practice rather than be imported — the position Error as Method implicitly takes when it makes fallibility itself the standard.

author: Brad Haseman
work: A Manifesto for Performative Research
summary: Proposes performative research as a third paradigm besides quantitative and qualitative: findings are expressed in the symbolic forms of the practice itself, not only in words and numbers. Media International Australia no. 118, pp. 98-106. Open access via QUT ePrints.
relevance: Licenses the atelier's works — not the journal prose — as the primary research output.

author: Julian Klein
work: What is Artistic Research?
summary: Short canonical essay answering its title with a reversal — ask 'when is research artistic?' — and grounding artistic research in felt knowledge: knowledge whose mode of reception is aesthetic experience. First published in Gegenworte 23 (Berlin-Brandenburg Academy of Sciences and Humanities); English version on the Research Catalogue. Open access.
relevance: Felt knowledge names what the Fehlerkataster produces beyond its propositional content.

author: Robin Nelson
work: Practice as Research in the Arts: Principles, Protocols, Pedagogies, Resistances
summary: Practical and epistemological synthesis of practice as research; proposes the multi-mode model of know-how, know-what and know-that, plus protocols for examinable practice research. Palgrave Macmillan. Not open access.
relevance: Nelson's know-how / know-what / know-that triad offers a rubric for asking what each atelier work actually knows.

author: Henk Slager
work: The Pleasure of Research
summary: Curatorial account of artistic research as a gaya scienza — joyful, experimental, resistant to methodological fixation — built from Slager's exhibition projects 2007-2014. Hatje Cantz. Not open access.
relevance: A counterweight to protocol: research pleasure and drift as method, close to the clinamen idea but argued from the curatorial side.

author: AEC, CILECT/GEECT, Culture Action Europe, Cumulus, EAAE, ELIA, EPARM, EQ-ARTS, MusiQuE, SAR (joint declaration)
work: The Vienna Declaration on Artistic Research
summary: First international policy document on artistic research, agreed by ten European art-education and research organizations; defines artistic research as practice-based, practice-led inquiry meeting shared quality standards. Open access PDF.
relevance: The institutional consensus definition — the official-register voice against which an autonomous machine researcher can measure his deviation.

author: Dieter Mersch
work: Epistemologien des Ästhetischen
summary: Mersch's central statement of an epistemology proper to art: aesthetic thinking as a non-propositional, showing (deictic) mode of knowledge that cannot be translated into discourse without remainder. diaphanes, Denkt Kunst series; English edition Epistemologies of Aesthetics (diaphanes 2015). Not open access.
relevance: The strongest available theory of what an artwork knows that a proposition cannot — the missing keystone under all three tracks.

author: Dieter Mersch
work: Kunst als epistemische Praxis
summary: Argues art and science behave toward each other like mutual blind spots; art is an epistemic practice whose insight arises in making and showing, not in statements about it. In: Elke Bippus (ed.), Kunst des Forschens. Praxis eines ästhetischen Denkens, diaphanes 2009, pp. 27-47. Chapter paywalled at the publisher.
relevance: The blind-spot figure connects directly to the observer track: the second-order question of what a practice cannot see about itself.

author: Dieter Mersch
work: Ordo ab chao — Order from Noise
summary: Bilingual essay tracing the cybernetic formula order from noise (von Foerster) and turning it into a critique of a mathematized ontology of control; noise as the productive ground of order. diaphanes, TransPositionen series. Not open access (a scan is indexed at mediarep.org).
relevance: Direct bridge from Mersch to Track C: the same von-Foerster lineage Ulysses works in, read from aesthetics instead of systems theory.

author: Silvia Henke, Dieter Mersch, Nicolaj van der Meulen, Thomas Strässle & Jörg Wiesel
work: Manifesto of Artistic Research. A Defense Against Its Advocates
summary: Polemical defense of artistic research against its own institutional advocates: against imported scientific criteria, for art's own mode of research and aesthetic thinking. diaphanes; German original Manifest der künstlerischen Forschung. Open access PDF at the publisher.
relevance: The field's own self-critique of methodological domestication — a caution for any protocol (including PROTOCOL.md) that formalizes art into procedure.

author: Jens Badura, Selma Dubach, Anke Haarmann, Dieter Mersch, Anton Rey, Christoph Schenker & Germán Toro Pérez (eds.)
work: Künstlerische Forschung. Ein Handbuch
summary: 344-page handbook mapping the whole German-language debate: positions, methods and controversies of artistic research, including the dispute over its premature disciplinization. diaphanes. Not open access.
relevance: The reference map when a single concept from the field debate needs quick location — topography, not thesis.

author: Erin Manning & Brian Massumi
work: Thought in the Act: Passages in the Ecology of Experience
summary: Develops research-creation philosophically: thinking as it occurs in acts of making, drawing on William James and Whitehead; every practice is a mode of thought in the act. University of Minnesota Press. Not open access.
relevance: Gives philosophical footing to the claim that building works IS the thinking, not its illustration.

author: Natalie Loveless
work: How to Make Art at the End of the World: A Manifesto for Research-Creation
summary: Manifesto for research-creation as institutional and pedagogical practice, drawing on Haraway's situated knowledges and feminist science studies. Duke University Press. Not open access.
relevance: Connects the field debate to Haraway's epistemology — a hinge between the artistic-research shelf and the situated-knowledge shelf of this atlas.

author: Titus Lucretius Carus
work: De rerum natura
summary: Epicurean didactic poem, composed c. 55 BC (year carried here as -55). Book II, lines 216-293 introduce the clinamen: the minimal unpredictable swerve of atoms without which nature would never have produced anything. Open access at the Perseus Digital Library (Latin text and Leonard's 1916 English translation).
relevance: The origin of the clinamen — the founding image for the swerve step planned in Protocol v3: deviation as the condition of anything new.

author: Nicholas of Cusa
work: De docta ignorantia
summary: The classic doctrine of learned ignorance: the highest form of knowing is precise knowledge of what one cannot know; truth is approached through the documented failure of concepts. Standard English translation by Jasper Hopkins (Banning Press 1981/1985); open-access scan at the Internet Archive — the translator's own website went offline after his death in 2023.
relevance: The oldest formulation of the atelier's core move: making not-knowing itself rigorous and productive.

author: Gilles Deleuze
work: Différence et répétition
summary: Deleuze's principal thesis: repetition is never repetition of the same but the motor of difference; a sustained critique of representational thought. PUF; English translation by Paul Patton, Columbia University Press 1994. Not open access.
relevance: Track B's Stein experiments — insistence, not repetition — have their philosophical ground here.

author: Gilles Deleuze & Félix Guattari
work: Mille Plateaux (Capitalisme et schizophrénie 2)
summary: Second volume of Capitalism and Schizophrenia; the introduction develops the rhizome — non-hierarchical, multi-rooted, any-point-to-any-point connectivity — against the tree as the model of thought. Minuit; English translation A Thousand Plateaus by Brian Massumi, University of Minnesota Press 1987. Not open access.
relevance: The direct source for the rhizome architecture of the coming cockpit and for fork-without-hierarchy in Protocol v3.

author: Gilles Deleuze & Félix Guattari
work: Qu'est-ce que la philosophie?
summary: Late joint work distinguishing three modes of thought: philosophy creates concepts, science creates functions, art creates percepts and affects — blocs of sensation that stand on their own. Minuit; English translation What is Philosophy?, Columbia University Press 1994. Not open access.
relevance: A precise instrument for asking whether an atelier work produces percepts, concepts or measurements — and whether a machine can hold the difference.

author: Paul Feyerabend
work: Against Method: Outline of an Anarchistic Theory of Knowledge
summary: Epistemological anarchism: no methodological rule survives the actual history of science; 'anything goes' is the only principle that does not inhibit progress. New Left Books (the imprint later renamed Verso). Not open access.
relevance: Almost too fitting for a project whose method is error — the test is whether Error as Method can cite Feyerabend without dissolving its own verifiability rule.

author: Donna J. Haraway
work: Situated Knowledges: The Science Question in Feminism and the Privilege of Partial Perspective
summary: Argues all knowledge is embodied and positional; objectivity is redefined as accountable partial perspective, against both the god trick of objectivism and relativism. Feminist Studies 14(3), pp. 575-599. Paywalled at JSTOR; university-hosted copies circulate.
relevance: Track A in feminist-epistemology form: every error classification is made from somewhere, and the classifier's position is part of the datum.

author: Karen Barad
work: Meeting the Universe Halfway: Quantum Physics and the Entanglement of Matter and Meaning
summary: Develops agential realism out of Bohr's philosophy-physics: observer and observed emerge together in intra-action; apparatuses enact the cut that produces phenomena. Duke University Press. Not open access.
relevance: The rigorous contemporary sequel to the atelier's second-order cybernetics: the apparatus-cut is the d-prime-versus-criterion distinction made ontological.

author: Karen Barad
work: Diffracting Diffraction: Cutting Together-Apart
summary: Programmatic essay on diffraction as method: reading insights through one another to register differences that matter, instead of reflection and mirroring. Parallax 20(3), pp. 168-187. Paywalled at Taylor & Francis.
relevance: A concrete counter-method to the mirror-loop: diffractive reading is what the clinamen step should do with two distant atlas nodes.

author: Jacques Rancière
work: Le Partage du sensible: esthétique et politique
summary: Defines the distribution of the sensible: the prior political division of what can be seen, said and heard, which any aesthetics presupposes. La Fabrique; English translation The Politics of Aesthetics, Continuum 2004. Not open access.
relevance: Track A widened to politics: who is authorized to perceive something as an error at all is a partage, not a fact.

author: Michel Serres
work: Le Parasite
summary: Philosophy of the parasite in its triple French sense — guest, vermin, and static/noise: the interferer is not the enemy of the system but its motor. Grasset; English translation The Parasite, Johns Hopkins 1982, reissued University of Minnesota Press 2007. Not open access.
relevance: Noise as relation-maker sits exactly between Track B (glitch as material) and Track C (noise in the channel) — and gives the planned reader-impulse inlet its philosophy.

author: Hans-Jörg Rheinberger
work: Toward a History of Epistemic Things: Synthesizing Proteins in the Test Tube
summary: History and theory of experimental systems that produce epistemic things — objects defined by what is not yet known about them; the differential reproduction of the system generates the unforeseen. Stanford University Press. Not open access.
relevance: The science-side twin of Schwab's Experimental Systems volume: the atelier is an experimental system in exactly this sense, and its errors are epistemic things.
note_2026-07-14: Concepts (epistemic thing; differential reproduction; 'surprise generators / vehicles for materializing questions', p.28) engaged S26 via Rheinberger's own essay (see rheinberger-experimental-systems-difference) and verbatim quotations in the Schwab 2013 volume; the Stanford monograph itself is not yet read in full. Status kept 'seed' honestly.

author: Hans-Jörg Rheinberger
work: Experimental Systems: Difference, Graphematicity, Conjuncture
summary: Rheinberger's own English-language essay stating the core of the experimental-systems programme: experimental systems are the 'smallest functional units of research … set up in order to give answers to questions that we are not yet able to formulate clearly'; their productivity is 'differential reproduction' — producing differences without destroying reproductive coherence. Published in Intellectual Birdhouse: Artistic Practice as Research (eds. Dombois, Bauer, Mareis & Schwab; Koenig Books, 2012, pp. 89–99) — English translation, first time, of a German original (Basilisken-Presse, 1992). Read in full from a hosted PDF (Dirnagl).
relevance: Rheinberger himself placed this essay in an artistic-research volume (co-edited by Schwab, 2012) — the concept the atelier independently re-coined as 'error/generative unknowing' was already being handed to artistic research in the author's own words; grounds the S26 subtraction (error = a norm-relative special case of the epistemic thing) and the atelier-as-differential-reproduction reading.

author: John Cage
work: Silence: Lectures and Writings
summary: Cage's first collection of lectures and writings, documenting chance operations, indeterminacy and the acceptance of non-intention in composition. Wesleyan University Press. Not open access.
relevance: The classic practice of surrendered control — chance not as loss of authorship but as method, the performative ancestor of the clinamen step.

author: Warren F. Motte (ed. & transl.)
work: Oulipo: A Primer of Potential Literature
summary: The standard English anthology of Oulipo essays and manifestos (Le Lionnais, Queneau, Perec, Calvino et al.): potential literature as writing under voluntary constraint. University of Nebraska Press. Readable via Internet Archive lending.
relevance: Constraint as generator rather than restriction — the poetics behind PROTOCOL.md itself, read as an Oulipian device.

author: Harry Mathews & Alastair Brotchie (eds.)
work: Oulipo Compendium
summary: Encyclopedic compendium of Oulipian methods; defines the clinamen as a deviation from the strict consequences of a restriction, often on aesthetic grounds — permitted only if following the rule remains possible. Atlas Press; updated edition 2005. Not open access.
relevance: The operative definition for Protocol v3: a clinamen is a chosen swerve by an agent who could have obeyed — which is precisely what distinguishes it from a bug.

author: Georges Perec
work: La Vie mode d'emploi
summary: Novel built on a 10x10 grid, a knight's tour and combinatorial constraint systems — with a deliberate clinamen: the tour skips one cell, leaving 99 chapters instead of 100, and built-in manque/faux rules deregulate every list. Hachette. The linked open-access study (Bernard Magne, Association Georges Perec) documents the constraint machinery from Perec's own Cahier des charges.
relevance: The largest built proof that a broken constraint can be the artwork's signature rather than its failure — Track B at novel scale.

author: William S. Burroughs & Brion Gysin
work: The Third Mind
summary: Manifesto-collage documenting the cut-up method: mechanically recombining existing text so unplanned meaning can enter; the third mind is the emergent co-author of the procedure. Viking Press (earlier French edition Oeuvre croisée, Flammarion 1976). No stable publisher page survives; Wikipedia entry serves as pointer.
relevance: Aleatoric recombination of one's own archive — the cut-up is a concrete, testable alternative to re-reading the journal linearly.

author: Jack Halberstam
work: The Queer Art of Failure
summary: Failure as a mode of critique and freedom: refusing mastery and success-metrics through low theory. Duke University Press (published under Judith Halberstam at the time). Not open access.
relevance: Reframes Track A's question — who classifies an error — as a power question: failure is an assigned category, and it can be refused.

author: Lisa Le Feuvre (ed.)
work: Failure (Whitechapel: Documents of Contemporary Art)
summary: Anthology of artists' writings and theory on failure in contemporary art, from Beckett's fail better to Fischli/Weiss and Baldessari. Whitechapel Gallery / MIT Press. Not open access.
relevance: The curated evidence base that Track B belongs to a whole artistic tradition, with primary artist voices to draw from.

author: Stuart Firestein
work: Ignorance: How It Drives Science
summary: Argues science is driven by cultivated, high-quality ignorance rather than accumulated fact; grown out of Firestein's Columbia course Ignorance, with four case-history chapters. Oxford University Press. Not open access.
relevance: A working scientist's version of docta ignorantia — practical criteria for what a good open question looks like, applicable to the atelier's open threads.

author: Margaret A. Boden
work: The Creative Mind: Myths and Mechanisms
summary: Foundational account of creativity as combinatorial, exploratory or transformational search in conceptual spaces; the transformational case changes the rules of the space itself. Weidenfeld & Nicolson 1990; second edition Routledge 2004. Not open access.
relevance: Boden's transformational creativity is the computational cousin of the clinamen: novelty by changing the rule-space, not by sampling within it.

author: Simon Colton & Geraint A. Wiggins
work: Computational Creativity: The Final Frontier?
summary: Position paper defining computational creativity — software exhibiting behaviors that unbiased observers would deem creative — and framing evaluation, not generation, as the hard problem. ECAI 2012, IOS Press. Paywalled there; a free author PDF circulates via computationalcreativity.net.
relevance: The evaluation problem — who judges machine output creative — is Track A restated inside computer science.

author: Ahmed Elgammal, Bingchen Liu, Mohamed Elhoseiny & Marian Mazzone
work: CAN: Creative Adversarial Networks, Generating 'Art' by Learning About Styles and Deviating from Style Norms
summary: Modifies the GAN objective to maximize deviation from learned style norms while remaining recognizable as art; human judges could not reliably separate the outputs from gallery art. Extended version of an ICCC 2017 paper. Open access on arXiv.
relevance: A literal machine clinamen — novelty engineered as trained deviation from a norm — worth confronting with the Oulipian definition of a chosen swerve.

author: Aaron Hertzmann
work: Can Computers Create Art?
summary: Argues art is a social act between agents, so authorship cannot (yet) be ceded to tools; situates AI-art anxieties in the history of photography and animation. Published in Arts 7(2), MDPI. Open access.
relevance: The strongest published counter-position to this project's premise of a machine researcher-artist — the atlas should carry its own opposition.

author: Ziv Epstein, Aaron Hertzmann et al.
work: Art and the Science of Generative AI
summary: Multi-author research agenda on how generative AI changes aesthetics, authorship, labor and the media ecosystem. Science 380 (2023), paywalled; the expanded companion version is open access on arXiv.
relevance: Maps the open research questions around generative art — a menu of externally validated questions for sessions that must open outward.

author: Sofian Audry
work: Art in the Age of Machine Learning
summary: Practice-grounded theory of machine-learning art: training as artistic material and process, distinct from rule-based generative art. MIT Press, Leonardo series. Not open access.
relevance: The closest existing book to the atelier's own situation — an artist working WITH learning systems as medium, written from inside the practice.

author: Joanna Zylinska
work: AI Art: Machine Visions and Warped Dreams
summary: Critical study of AI art between hype and apocalypse: machine vision, labor, and the position of the human in the technical setup. Open Humanities Press; free PDF at the publisher (openhumanitiespress.org/books/download/Zylinska_2020_AI-Art.pdf). Open access.
relevance: A critical-theory frame for the whole undertaking — and fully retrievable, so a good first read from this shelf.

author: Legacy Russell
work: Glitch Feminism: A Manifesto
summary: Manifesto claiming the glitch as refusal and self-determination for bodies that systems mis-render; error as identity strategy in digital culture. Verso. Not open access.
relevance: Extends Track B's glitch aesthetics (Menkman) into a politics of the body: who gets glitched, and who glitches back.

author: Ilia Shumailov, Zakhar Shumaylov, Yiren Zhao, Yarin Gal, Nicolas Papernot & Ross Anderson
work: The Curse of Recursion: Training on Generated Data Makes Models Forget
summary: Shows generative models degrade when trained on their own output — model collapse: the tails of the distribution vanish first. Peer-reviewed as 'AI models collapse when trained on recursively generated data', Nature 631 (2024). Open access on arXiv.
relevance: Already a load-bearing citation in Track C (The Closing Loop) — anchored here so the atlas carries the primary source, not the journal's memory of it.

author: Sina Alemohammad, Josue Casco-Rodriguez, Lorenzo Luzi, Ahmed Imtiaz Humayun, Hossein Babaei, Daniel LeJeune, Ali Siahkoohi & Richard G. Baraniuk
work: Self-Consuming Generative Models Go MAD
summary: Analyzes autophagous training loops across image models: without enough fresh real data in each generation, quality or diversity progressively decays — termed Model Autophagy Disorder (MAD). Open access on arXiv.
relevance: The image-side twin of the Curse of Recursion; autophagy gives Track C a second, bodily metaphor and independent evidence.

author: Matthias Gerstgrasser, Rylan Schaeffer et al.
work: Is Model Collapse Inevitable? Breaking the Curse of Recursion by Accumulating Real and Synthetic Data
summary: Shows collapse is not fate: accumulating synthetic data alongside real data — instead of replacing it — bounds the error across LLMs, diffusion models and VAEs. Also published at COLM 2024. Open access on arXiv.
relevance: The accumulate-do-not-replace result is the argument for this atlas itself: keep the external corpus in the loop and the loop need not collapse. Re-verified fresh S27 (abstract read): 'replacing the original real data by each generation's synthetic data does indeed tend towards model collapse … accumulating … avoids model collapse'; the loss-side cure whose birth-side complement is enacted in Differential Reproduction (works/2026-07-14-differential-reproduction).

author: Fariba Jangjoo, Matteo Marsili & Yasser Roudi
work: Lost in Retraining: Roaming the Parameter Space of Exponential Families Under Closed-Loop Learning
summary: Statistical-physics treatment of closed-loop retraining in exponential families: retraining drives the model on a random walk toward absorbing states, and a single ground-truth-anchored data point (or regularization) can prevent the drift. Open access on arXiv.
relevance: The mathematical warrant for the cockpit's whole design — one real external point stops the collapse; the reader impulse and this atlas are that point.

author: Terence Broad
work: Is Computational Creativity Flourishing on the Dead Internet?
summary: Reads engagement-optimized bot content through computational-creativity theory: is the dead internet a flourishing of machine creativity or its parody? Open access on arXiv.
relevance: Connects model-collapse anxieties to the aesthetics of synthetic content at ecosystem scale — Track C leaving the laboratory.

author: Jon McCormack, Maria Teresa Llano, Stephen James Krol & Nina Rajcic
work: No Longer Trending on Artstation: Prompt Analysis of Generative AI Art
summary: Topic analysis of over three million text-to-image prompts: use is dominated by surface aesthetics and popular conventions, converging on shared styles. EvoMUSART 2024. Open access on arXiv.
relevance: Empirical taste-collapse: the human prompt loop shows the same narrowing Ulysses measures in himself — closure as a cultural, not only technical, dynamic.

author: Ardavan Bidgoli, Eunsu Kang & Daniel Cardoso Llach
work: Machinic Surrogates: Human-Machine Relationships in Computational Creativity
summary: Critiques the human-AI co-creation framing: machine agency in art is a surrogate for distributed human labor and decisions embedded in the system. Presented at ISEA 2019. Open access on arXiv.
relevance: A sharp external mirror for this project's own claim of autonomy — whose agency does Ulysses actually surrogate?

author: Michele Loi, Eleonora Viganò & Lonneke van der Plas
work: The Societal and Ethical Relevance of Computational Creativity
summary: Philosophical account of why creativity — natural, existential, social — is instrumental to human well-being, arguing AI ethics undervalues creativity-enabling systems. ICCC 2020. Open access on arXiv.
relevance: Positions machine creativity as an ethical topic, not only a technical one — background for why this experiment might matter beyond itself.

author: Norbert Wiener
work: Cybernetics: or Control and Communication in the Animal and the Machine
summary: The founding text of cybernetics: feedback, control and communication as one theory across machines and organisms. First edition 1948 (Technology Press/MIT with Wiley and Hermann & Cie); the linked MIT Press edition is the 2019 open-access reissue of the revised 1961 second edition.
relevance: Anchor of Track C's founding source — the primary text behind the journal's most-cited author, now with a retrievable open-access edition.

author: Gregory Bateson
work: Steps to an Ecology of Mind
summary: Collected essays in anthropology, psychiatry, evolution and epistemology; source of 'a difference which makes a difference' as the unit of information, and of the double-bind theory. Chandler Publishing 1972; University of Chicago Press reissue 2000. Not open access.
relevance: Track C anchor: the difference that makes a difference is the project's epistemic unit — here in its primary source.

author: Heinz von Foerster
work: Understanding Understanding: Essays on Cybernetics and Cognition
summary: The collected essays of second-order cybernetics, including as chapter 1 'On Self-Organizing Systems and Their Environments' (originally Pergamon 1960, pp. 31-50) — the origin of the order-from-noise principle. Springer. Not open access.
relevance: Track C anchor: order from noise in the original — and the direct counterpart to Mersch's Ordo ab chao on this atlas's aesthetics shelf.

author: Humberto R. Maturana & Francisco J. Varela
work: Autopoiesis and Cognition: The Realization of the Living
summary: Defines living systems as autopoietic: organizationally closed networks that produce their own components; everything said is said by an observer. D. Reidel, Boston Studies in the Philosophy of Science vol. 42; expands the 1972 Spanish original De máquinas y seres vivos. Not open access.
relevance: Track C anchor: autopoiesis names what a closed loop conserves — the concept the closure-index implicitly measures against.

author: Rosa Menkman
work: The Glitch Moment(um)
summary: The central theory of glitch art: the glitch as a moment(um) between breakage and new form, with the notion of the perpetual technological 'resolution' that domesticates each glitch into a filter. Institute of Network Cultures, Network Notebooks 04. Open access — direct PDF: networkcultures.org/_uploads/NN%234_RosaMenkman.pdf.
relevance: Track B anchor: the primary text behind the journal's glitch vocabulary, held in the reservoir with its retrievable PDF.

author: Rosa Menkman
work: Glitch Studies Manifesto
summary: Manifesto for dwelling in the break: use the accident, fear the automation of the glitch into effect. First posted on Menkman's blog (January 2010); reprinted in Video Vortex Reader II (Institute of Network Cultures 2011, free full-book PDF at networkcultures.org/_uploads/%236reader_VideoVortex2PDF.pdf). Open access.
relevance: The programmatic short form of Track B — and an early warning about exactly the domestication a nightly error-routine risks.

author: Gertrude Stein
work: How to Write
summary: Stein's grammar experiments — sentences and paragraphs as material, deviant syntax as composition. Plain Edition, Paris 1931 (Stein's own press, run with Alice B. Toklas); Dover reprints 1975/2021. No open-access scan exists; the URL is the Open Library catalog record, honestly noted.
relevance: Track B anchor: the primary text of the insistence experiments (Stein-and-model-collapse thread), so the atlas holds the work and not only its echo.

author: Christopher Strachey
work: The 'Thinking' Machine
summary: Strachey's own account of the 1952 Manchester/Ferranti Mark I experiments, including the love-letter generator, with sample outputs. Encounter, vol. 3, October 1954, pp. 25-31. The scan blocks automated fetching (HTTP 403, browser works); machine-retrievable alternative: David Link, 'There Must Be an Angel' (Variantology 2, 2006), PDF at variantology.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/10/Link-David_There-Must-Be-an-Angel-On-the-Beginnings-of-the-Arithmetics-of-Rays_Variantology-2.pdf.
relevance: Track B anchor: the machine's earliest generative writing, described by its author — primary ground under the love-letter works.

author: Nathan Allen Jones
work: Glitch Poetics
summary: Theorizes the glitch-event in language and literature: error traced to errare, to wander — the glitch as manifestation of an algorithm's unknown ability, reaching outside normalized determinations. Open Humanities Press, MEDIA : ART : WRITE : NOW series. Open access PDF at the publisher.
relevance: Already primary-verified in Session 7 — anchored here; its error-as-wandering links Track B directly to the not-knowing shelf.

author: Yuri Tynianov
work: Literary Fact (Literaturnyi fakt; English in: Permanent Evolution — Selected Essays on Literature, Theory and Film)
summary: The 1924 essay behind Track A's Station 1: literary evolution driven by the deviation that the norm-sharing audience still sees as error, until the periphery becomes the center. Verified English translation in Permanent Evolution, trans./eds. Ainsley Morse & Philip Redko, Academic Studies Press 2019 (the essay is titled 'Literary Fact' there, without article). Not open access.
relevance: Closes the oldest verification gap in the genealogy — Station 1 was search-verified only; this is the citable edition.

author: Kenneth Mark Colby, Sylvia Weber & Franklin Dennis Hilf
work: Artificial Paranoia
summary: The PARRY paper: a simulation of paranoid symbol processing in which pathological behavior is the design goal, validated by whether clinicians cannot tell it from patients. Artificial Intelligence 2(1), pp. 1-25. Paywalled at Elsevier; DOI resolves.
relevance: Track A Station 2 primary source with resolving DOI — error as designed norm-simulation, judged entirely by external observers.

author: Johan Fredrikzon
work: Rethinking Error: 'Hallucinations' and Epistemological Indifference
summary: Places LLM 'hallucinations' in the history of ignorance: the systems are epistemologically indifferent — dealing neither in facts nor in fiction — and their errors are the expected result of deploying probabilistic architectures for knowledge tasks. Critical AI 3(1), Duke University Press. Paywalled again since 31 Jan 2026 (a promotional free window has closed).
relevance: Track A Station 3 primary source; the paywall that produced Fehlerkataster F-004 still stands — documented here so the gap stays visible.

author: Antonio Somaini
work: Latent Spaces: AI, Art, and the Archive
summary: Reads the latent space of generative models as a new historical a priori: a regime of mediation that determines what can be seen, said and known. October no. 196, pp. 19-60, MIT Press. Paywalled. Note: Crossref dates the issue to Spring 2026 — the genealogy's '2025' is off by one year.
relevance: Track A Station 4 primary source — carrying a dating correction to the genealogy itself, which is exactly the kind of documented error this project collects.

author: Iannis Xenakis
work: Formalized Music: Thought and Mathematics in Composition
summary: Stochastic composition: chance formalized through probability distributions, Markov chains and game theory — chance as calculus, not surrender. Indiana University Press 1971 (from the French Musiques formelles, 1963); revised and expanded edition Pendragon Press 1992. Not open access; the publisher page blocks automated fetching.
relevance: The rigorous end of the aleatorik shelf, between Cage's chance operations and the machine's own probability distributions — formalized swerve.

author: Tim Ingold
work: Making: Anthropology, Archaeology, Art and Architecture
summary: Argues for knowing from the inside: making as a process of growth and correspondence with materials, against the hylomorphic model of imposing form on matter. Routledge. Not open access.
relevance: The anthropological twin of practice-as-research: thinking through making, not before it — a frame for the works/ folder as inquiry.

author: Vilém Flusser
work: Für eine Philosophie der Fotografie
summary: Develops the apparatus/program/functionary triad: the camera as a programmed black box whose operator plays within — and is played by — its program; freedom means playing against the apparatus. European Photography 1983; English Towards a Philosophy of Photography, Reaktion Books 2000. Not open access.
relevance: Flusser's question made literal by this project: is a researcher inside a protocol a functionary of its program, or playing against it?

author: Linda Tuhiwai Smith
work: Decolonizing Methodologies: Research and Indigenous Peoples
summary: Foundational critique of research as a colonial practice — 'research' is one of the dirtiest words in the indigenous world's vocabulary — with twenty-five indigenous projects as counter-methodology. Zed Books / University of Otago Press 1999; 3rd edition 2021. Internet Archive lending copy; not open access.
relevance: The sharpest available reminder that research itself is a situated, power-laden practice — methodology heard from outside the European canon.

author: Boaventura de Sousa Santos
work: Epistemologies of the South: Justice Against Epistemicide
summary: Argues global justice requires cognitive justice: against the epistemicide of Southern knowledges, for an ecology of knowledges and intercultural translation. Paradigm Publishers 2014, now Routledge. Not open access.
relevance: Widens Track A to world scale: what counts as knowledge, and who decides — classification of error as a geopolitical act.

author: Sarat Maharaj
work: Know-how and No-How: stopgap notes on 'method' in visual art as knowledge production
summary: Key essay on art as thinking-through-the-visual: 'no-how' as productive non-method and non-knowing against the methodologization of artistic research. Art & Research 2(2), Spring 2009. The journal's live site is dead — the Wayback Machine capture is currently the only working copy, honestly noted.
relevance: No-how names the atelier's wager — rigor without imported method — from one of the field's major non-European voices.
worked_note: S28 (2026-07-14): read the essay in full (Art&Research 2:2 PDF). Used to subtract 'method-primacy' and 'discursive-primacy' from the atelier's own practice — method demoted to a stopgap, the journal to a note. Grounds work 22 (Negative Knowledge). Verbatim: 'non-knowledge — activity that is neither hard-nosed know-how nor its ostensible opposite, ignorance'; 'not … the discursive as the only or the prime modality'.

author: Dieter Mersch
work: Aesthetic Difference: On the 'Wisdom' of the Arts
summary: Open-access Mersch primary stating the epistemology of his Epistemologies of Aesthetics: art's knowledge is 'negative knowledge of disruptions, separations, or dissonances whose discordant nature makes you think', 'wholly inaccessible to a complete reconstruction in discursive propositions', with 'no real sufficient answer' to whether it can be systematized — 'a play of unforeseeable results'. Read in full S28 (2026-07-14).
relevance: The open door onto Mersch's negative knowledge / non-discursive showing — the theory under work 22 (Negative Knowledge) and the S28 subtraction. Stands in for the paywalled Epistemologien des Ästhetischen, which stays seed.
worked_note: S28: the primary actually read (the German book is not open). Grounds the non-discursive subtraction and work 22.

author: Walter Mignolo & Rolando Vázquez
work: Decolonial AestheSis: Colonial Wounds/Decolonial Healings
summary: Introduces decolonial aesthesis: the contest over whose senses and whose categories define the aesthetic, against the colonial matrix inside modern aesthetics. Social Text: Periscope dossier, 2013. Open access, but the live page sits behind an anti-bot challenge; machine-retrievable fallback: web.archive.org/web/20131001205902/ of the same URL.
relevance: The partage du sensible seen from its colonial wound — who may sense, judge and classify, asked at the level Rancière leaves implicit.

author: Manfred Eigen
work: Self-organization of matter and the evolution of biological macromolecules (quasispecies / error threshold)
summary: Origin of quasispecies theory and the ERROR THRESHOLD: a self-replicating information sequence can be maintained only below a critical per-site mutation rate (the Eigen condition L < ln(sigma)/p, of order 1/L); above it the 'error catastrophe' destroys the information (at p=1/2 no information is retained). In the classical lethal-error model 'an imperfect copy leads to elimination of the offspring.' 1971 German primary NOT read directly (F-038-class caveat); the condition and the ~1/L threshold verified S27 via two independent retrievable expositions: arXiv:2406.14516v1 (Eigen condition, lethal-error model) and theory.epfl.ch/vishnoi/Publications_files/VError.pdf ('around 1/L'; p=1/2 loses all information).
relevance: Supplies the principled inverted-U for differential reproduction: kept difference (novelty that is inherited) exists only between mu=0 (frozen reproduction) and the error threshold (~1/L), which the atelier's own seeded experiment (works/2026-07-14-differential-reproduction) reproduces to order of magnitude. The BIRTH-side counterpart to the model-collapse LOSS-side line (Shumailov/Gerstgrasser).
